draft.introduction: Book V of Boethius’s Consolation of Philosophy (c. 524 CE) addresses the problem of free will and divine foreknowledge. Lady Philosophy argues that God’s knowledge is not temporal foresight but eternal presence: God sees all time in a single simultaneous act of vision.

translation.original_ref: Boethius, Consolatio Philosophiae V, Prose 6 (Moreschini, Teubner)
translation.text: God’s knowledge is not foreknowledge of future events but knowledge of a never-passing instant. For eternity is the complete, simultaneous, and perfect possession of unlimited life. God embraces all the infinite spaces of future and past in one present act of knowing. His simple gaze encompasses all things not as about to happen but as happening now, in his own eternal present.
translation.original_text: Aeternitas igitur est interminabilis vitae tota simul et perfecta possessio.

translator_notes.note: Boethius’s definition of eternity as tota simul et perfecta possessio (“the complete, simultaneous, and perfect possession” of unlimited life) became the standard definition in medieval theology. His solution to the free will problem—that divine knowledge is atemporal—was adopted by Aquinas and remains influential.
